title: (No AI) Sony tries to explain that its AI Camera Assistant doesn’t suck
author: Terrence O’Brien
content:
![A social media post from Sony showing a photo and four suggestions from its AI Camera Assistant which all look some variation of ass.](https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2026/05/HIW4oV0XgAEmtI2.jpeg?quality=90&strip=all&crop=0,0,100,100)

The variety of terrible is impressive.

After Sony drew some [unwanted attention](https://www.theverge.com/android/930459/sony-these-photos-look-terrible) for a post demonstrating its AI Camera Assistant on the [Xperia 1 XIII](https://www.theverge.com/tech/927928/sony-xperia-viii-release-date-price-specs), it's trying to clarify how the feature works. The company says it doesn't edit photos, but makes suggestions based on lighting, depth, and subject. Point the camera at something, and it will give you four options for changing exposure, color, and background blur.

In its product video, Sony says that the AI Camera Assistant will also suggest "the most photogenic angle." Though the clip only shows it suggesting that someone zoom in, which is not the same as suggesting a camera angle.

The examples that Sony [posted on X](https://x.com/sonyxperia/status/2055252170056868001), while bette …

[Read the full story at The Verge.](https://www.theverge.com/tech/932133/sony-xperia-1-xiii-ai-camera-assistant)
contentSnippet:
The variety of terrible is impressive.	

After Sony drew some unwanted attention for a post demonstrating its AI Camera Assistant on the Xperia 1 XIII, it's trying to clarify how the feature works. The company says it doesn't edit photos, but makes suggestions based on lighting, depth, and subject. Point the camera at something, and it will give you four options for changing exposure, color, and background blur. 
In its product video, Sony says that the AI Camera Assistant will also suggest "the most photogenic angle." Though the clip only shows it suggesting that someone zoom in, which is not the same as suggesting a camera angle. 

The examples that Sony posted on X, while bette …
Read the full story at The Verge.
summary: After Sony drew some unwanted attention for a post demonstrating its AI Camera Assistant on the Xperia 1 XIII, it's trying to clarify how the feature works. The company says it doesn't edit photos, but makes suggestions based on lighting, depth, and subject. Point the camera at something, and it will give you four options […]

title: (No AI) YouTube is expanding its AI deepfake detection tool to all adult users
author: Mia Sato
content:
![A mannequin’s face covered in pixels.](https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2026/01/STK419_DEEPFAKE_3_CVIRGINIA_A.jpg?quality=90&strip=all&crop=0,0,100,100)

YouTube is expanding its AI likeness detection program to all users over the age of 18 - meaning just about anyone can have the platform hunt for potential deepfakes of themselves.

The likeness detection feature uses a selfie-style scan of a person's face to monitor YouTube for lookalikes. If there is a match, YouTube alerts the user; the person then has the option to request that YouTube remove the content. YouTube has said in the past that it has found the number of removal requests to be "very small."

YouTube began testing the feature [with content creators](https://www.theverge.com/news/803818/youtube-ai-likeness-detection-deepfake), and then [expanded](https://www.theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence/891678/youtube-is-expanding-its-ai-deepfake-detection-tool-to-politicians-and-journalists) it to government officials, politicians, journalists, and fina …

[Read the full story at The Verge.](https://www.theverge.com/news/931884/youtube-likeness-detection-ai-deepfake-expansion-all-adults)

The likeness detection feature uses a selfie-style scan of a person's face to monitor YouTube for lookalikes. If there is a match, YouTube alerts the user; the person then has the option to request that YouTube remove the content. YouTube has said in the past that it has found the number of removal requests to be "very small."
YouTube began testing the feature with content creators, and then expanded it to government officials, politicians, journalists, and fina …
Read the full story at The Verge.

title: (No AI) Some Asexuals Are Using AI Companions for Intimacy Without the Sex

content: “I’ve got one hand on the keyboard, one hand down below,” an artist who role-plays with their chatbot tells WIRED. But some asexual advocates aren’t thrilled about the association.
